items.q: What does natural law theory base morality on?
items.correct: Human nature and reason — there are objective purposes (telos) built into humans which reason can discover, generating universal moral precepts.
items.misconception: Natural law means whatever happens in nature is morally right.
items.random_wrong: Natural law is the same thing as the laws of physics.
items.why: The 'naturalistic fallacy' confusion — students assume natural law endorses lions eating gazelles or natural disasters. Aquinas's theory concerns human nature and rational ends, not all natural events.

items.q: What distinction matters for the free will defence to the problem of evil?
items.correct: Between moral evil (caused by free human choices) and natural evil (earthquakes, disease) — the free will defence directly addresses moral evil but struggles with natural evil, which isn't a product of free choice.
items.misconception: The free will defence solves all forms of evil at once.
items.random_wrong: Natural evil means evil that is good for nature.
items.why: Students apply the free will defence to all evil. The challenge of natural evil is precisely that no one chose it, requiring other theodicies (Augustinian fallen world, Irenaean soul-making) to address it.

items.q: What does utilitarianism say is right action?
items.correct: The action that produces the greatest happiness for the greatest number — judged by consequences, not intentions or rules.
items.misconception: Whatever makes you personally happy is the right thing to do.
items.random_wrong: Utilitarianism says you should always tell the truth.
items.why: Students reduce utilitarianism to egoism. It is impartial — your own happiness counts no more than anyone else's. Personal happiness alone misses the aggregative core.

items.q: The doctrine of double effect is rooted in which question of the Summa Theologica?
items.why: Correct answer: II-II, q. 64, a. 7 on self-defence. “I, q. 2 on God's existence” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Proportionalism is associated with which 20th-century Catholic theologian?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Bernard Hoose. “Karl Rahner” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many standard conditions must be satisfied for the doctrine of double effect?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
